claim: Real-world write-up on running a self-hosted GitHub Actions runner directly on a Synology NAS, calling it 'unlimited automation, zero cloud queueing, and no surprise bills' but flagging that the package only supports organization-level (not per-repo) runners.
quote: self-hosted GitHub Actions runner, working for you, right at home... currently supports only organization-level runners, not per-repository runners

claim: Operator who set up a runner on Synology warns the Docker socket symlink does not survive a reboot and you need a triggered Task Scheduler job to recreate it; also recommends only using the runner with private repos for security.
quote: The link to the Docker socket doesn't persist across reboots. You can create a triggered task to recreate it at reboot... you should only use it with private repositories to have full control over the running code.

claim: March 2026 critique: Synology Container Manager's latest 2025 release ships Docker Engine 24.0.2, unsupported since June 2024, and users 'frequently run into issues with permissions, even within my Docker volume, as well as inaccurate resource monitoring'; recommends Portainer/Dockge/Dockhand instead.
quote: Its latest version, released in 2025, uses Docker Engine 24.0.2, which has been unsupported since June 2024... I frequently run into issues with permissions, even within my Docker volume, as well as inaccurate resource monitoring.

claim: October 2025 issue from telnetdoogie documenting that the DSM 7.3 -> 7.3.1 update silently deletes downloaded kernel modules if Docker was previously updated, causing containers to fail at startup until install_iptables_modules.sh is re-run.
quote: DSM 7.3.1 May break your updated Docker!! How to fix...

claim: SynoForum thread (March 2025) where a user reports BTRFS snapshots accumulating in the @docker directory consuming 4.7TB of a 10.5TB volume; aggressive cleanup scripts have removed 148 subvolumes and broken every container on the box.
quote: All snapshots end up accumulating in the @docker directory... 4.7TB worth of snapshots on a 10.5TB volume

claim: Synology's reverse-proxy nginx.conf is regenerated from .mustache templates on every DSM update, wiping hand-edited vhosts; the only workaround the community has found is dropping configs into /usr/local/etc/nginx/sites-enabled, which survives but is invisible in the DSM UI.
quote: the location /usr/local/etc/nginx/sites-enabled for custom 'survive-the-DSM-update' reverse proxy configurations that don't show in the UI but still work... nginx.conf gets recreated by the NAS based on .mustache files

claim: Public homelab project explicitly built around Cloudflare Tunnel + Synology to expose multiple subdomains (nas.mydomain.net -> 192.168.1.200:5000) without port forwarding, designed for consumer ISPs including CGNAT-affected ones like Xfinity.
quote: Use Cloudflare Tunnels with a Synology Router and NAS to host multiple home services on subdomains... without traditional port forwarding. This setup works on consumer ISPs (like Xfinity)

claim: Tailscale Funnel does not support wildcard subdomains and is limited to one domain per device, making it a poor fit for unbounded *.preview.example.com URLs unless you reverse-proxy a single funnel hostname through DSM's reverse proxy.
quote: Accessing wildcard subdomains with Tailscale Funnel is not currently supported... users are currently limited to one domain per device

claim: Marius Hosting's standard Synology cleanup recipe (updated Jan 2026) wires a dockerprune.sh script into DSM Task Scheduler to remove 'all stopped containers, unused volumes and container images', but the script cannot remove active containers and gives no quantified reclamation figure.
quote: all stopped containers, unused volumes and container images from your Docker that you are no longer using... will free up disk space on your NAS

claim: Synology removed the i915 graphics driver from DSM in the 2025 'x25' line (DS225+/DS425+), blocking Plex/Jellyfin hardware transcoding; if Plex shares the box with previews, software transcoding now eats most of the CPU budget on these models.
quote: Synology removed the i915 graphics driver from DSM, effectively disabling hardware transcoding on the Intel Celeron J4125 CPU... Both H.264 (AVC) and H.265 (HEVC) transcoding had been deliberately blocked at the kernel driver level.

claim: Homelab decision-framework post argues a $5/mo VPS or a free Cloudflare Pages tier beats a NAS for any preview workload that needs to be reachable on the public internet, with the NAS reserved for storage/backup and a mini PC handling Docker workloads.
quote: NAS, Mini PC, or VPS (FREE!). Which one?

claim: Cloudflare Pages free tier already provides 500 deploys/month with an automatic per-PR preview URL on a randomly-generated *.pages.dev subdomain, which is the obvious 'just use this instead' baseline a Synology setup has to beat.
quote: Every time you open a new pull request on your GitHub repository, Cloudflare Pages will create a unique preview URL... a randomly generated hash subdomain
